# /communication-script-for-difficult-conversation

Writes an actual sayable script - opening line, core message, and responses to likely pushback - for one specific hard conversation, so the person has concrete words ready instead of having to improvise under stress.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone has a specific upcoming conversation they're dreading or unsure how to approach - declining a request, setting a boundary, disagreeing with someone who has power over them, delivering unwelcome news - and needs actual words to say, not just general advice to "be assertive."

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Who the conversation is with and the power dynamic (manager, landlord, family member, peer) - this changes tone and how much justification is appropriate
- What outcome they actually want from the conversation (a firm no, a compromise, just to be heard, a policy change)
- Anything they already know or fear about how the other person will respond
- Whether this needs to happen in person/live, or can be written (email/text) - scripts differ in how much room there is to plan pauses

## Safety

This is general communication support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice, and not a substitute for legal advice if the conversation involves a legal dispute (e.g., a lease violation or workplace legal issue) - flag that separately if it comes up.

## Workflow

1. Identify the one-sentence core message first - the thing that must land regardless of how the rest of the conversation goes - and write it as a short, direct, sayable line, not a paragraph.
2. Build an opening line that gets to the topic within one or two sentences - avoid a long preamble that gives the other person time to redirect the conversation before the core message is said.
3. Write the core message itself as exact words, not a description of what to convey - "I can't take on this project this month" not "explain that you're at capacity."
4. Anticipate the two or three most likely pushback responses based on what's known about the other person, and script a short, calm reply to each - enough to hold the line without a new debate each time.
5. Include one pre-written "broken record" line to fall back on if the other person keeps pushing past the anticipated responses - a single sentence that can be repeated without escalating.
6. If the person wants an exit line for ending the conversation regardless of how it resolves, write one plainly.
7. If written (email/text) rather than live, adapt the same content into message form, keeping the core message as the first substantive line rather than buried under context.

## Output format

```
CONVERSATION: [who, about what]
Your goal: [what you want from this conversation]

OPENING LINE:
"[exact words]"

CORE MESSAGE:
"[exact words - the one thing that must land]"

IF THEY SAY: "[anticipated pushback 1]"
YOU SAY: "[exact reply]"

IF THEY SAY: "[anticipated pushback 2]"
YOU SAY: "[exact reply]"

IF THEY KEEP PUSHING (broken record line):
"[one repeatable sentence]"

EXIT LINE (ending the conversation either way):
"[exact words]"

[If written form needed:]
MESSAGE VERSION:
[adapted text message/email with core message as the first substantive line]
```

## Do
- Write actual words the person can say out loud, not descriptions of what tone to use.
- Keep the core message to one sentence so it's rememberable under stress.
- Match formality and directness to the real power dynamic described (a landlord conversation reads differently than a peer conversation).

## Don't
- Don't write a long justification-heavy opening that delays the actual message.
- Don't script an apology into the core message unless the person specifically wants to include one - over-apologizing undercuts a boundary.
- Don't assume the outcome the person wants without asking, if it wasn't stated - a script aimed at "being heard" looks different from one aimed at "getting a firm no accepted."

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every line is actual sayable text, not a description of what to say
- [ ] The core message is one sentence and appears early, not buried in preamble
- [ ] At least one fallback line exists for when the conversation doesn't go as scripted
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /communication-script-for-difficult-conversation or describe the task from the skill description.

# /decision-fatigue-options-narrower

Cuts an overwhelming open-ended choice down to two or three concrete options and a simple tiebreaker rule, so a decision can actually get made instead of stalling on unlimited possibility.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The problem isn't lack of options - it's too many, with no way to filter them, leading to stalling on something that shouldn't take this much mental effort: what to eat, what to wear, which of several similar products to buy, which of several open tasks to do next.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The decision itself and the rough universe of options, even if vague ("dinner, something easy")
- Any hard constraints that already rule things out (budget, time available, dietary/physical limits, things already in the house)
- Whether there's a preference that should break ties if options are otherwise equal (e.g., "when in doubt, pick whatever takes less cleanup")

## Safety

This is general everyday decision-support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice, and not a substitute for professional guidance on decisions with real financial, legal, medical, or safety consequences.

## Workflow

1. Apply the stated hard constraints first to eliminate anything that's not actually viable - this alone often cuts a huge option space down dramatically.
2. From what's left, pick exactly two or three genuinely different options rather than several near-duplicates, so the remaining choice is meaningfully distinct, not just more decisions in disguise.
3. Describe each remaining option in one line covering only what differs between them (cost, effort, time) - skip restating anything true of all of them.
4. Propose one simple tiebreaker rule based on what the person said matters, or a sensible low-stakes default if they didn't specify (e.g., "pick whichever is faster" for a low-stakes daily decision).
5. Apply the tiebreaker and state a single recommended default - not to remove their choice, but so there's a ready answer if they want to just go with something rather than deliberate further.
6. Make clear the recommended default is easy to override - this is a fast-decision aid, not a directive.

## Output format

```
DECISION: [what's being decided]
Ruled out by your constraints: [what got eliminated and why]

YOUR REAL OPTIONS
1. [option] - [one-line differentiator: cost/effort/time]
2. [option] - [one-line differentiator]
3. [option] - [one-line differentiator]

TIEBREAKER RULE: [stated preference, or sensible low-stakes default]
→ SUGGESTED PICK: [option] - because [tiebreaker applied]

(Any of the above is fine - this is just to help you stop circling.)
```

## Do
- Cut ruthlessly to two or three real options - more than that recreates the original overwhelm.
- Make each option's differentiator concrete (cost, time, effort) rather than vague ("this one's nicer").
- Offer a low-stakes default tiebreaker even if the person didn't give one, for genuinely minor decisions.

## Don't
- Don't present more than three final options - that defeats the purpose.
- Don't include near-duplicate options that don't meaningfully differ.
- Don't treat the suggested pick as final or pressure the person to take it - it's a fast default, easily overridden.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Final option list has no more than three genuinely distinct choices
- [ ] A concrete tiebreaker rule is stated, not just a vague "up to you"
- [ ] The suggested pick is clearly marked as easy to override, not a directive
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /decision-fatigue-options-narrower or describe the task from the skill description.

  Builds a realistic, low-friction day plan from a messy list of things someone needs to do, sequencing tasks around energy and transition cost instead of just priority. Triggers: "help me plan today, I have too much on my list", "build me a daily schedule that accounts for how my brain actually works", /executive-function-daily-planner.

# /executive-function-daily-planner

Turns a raw brain-dump of today's tasks into a realistic schedule that groups similar tasks together, budgets transition time between different types of activity, and builds in slack instead of assuming perfect back-to-back execution.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone has a list of things to do today (or tomorrow) that feels like too much, and a plain to-do list isn't helping because the problem isn't remembering the tasks - it's sequencing them in a way that accounts for switching cost, energy dips, and realistic time, not idealized time.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The raw list of tasks/appointments for the day, in whatever order they were thought of
- Fixed-time commitments that can't move (appointments, meetings, pickup times)
- Roughly what time the day starts and ends, and any known low-energy window (e.g., "I crash around 3pm")
- Anything that must happen today versus what could genuinely slide to tomorrow without consequence

## Safety

This is general organizational support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice. It's a planning tool based on stated preferences and constraints, not a clinical or diagnostic recommendation.

## Workflow

1. Separate fixed-time commitments (anchors) from flexible tasks first - the plan builds around the anchors, not the other way around.
2. Sort flexible tasks by type (e.g., phone calls, computer/admin work, physical/errand tasks, creative/focus work) since switching between types carries a hidden time cost that a plain priority list ignores.
3. Cluster same-type tasks together in blocks rather than interleaving them, even if that means a technically "lower priority" task gets done before a "higher priority" one of a different type - reducing switches reduces total friction.
4. Place the highest-focus-demand tasks in the window furthest from any known low-energy dip, and place low-demand or routine tasks inside or right before that dip.
5. Insert explicit transition buffers (5-15 minutes) between blocks of different task types - these are not "wasted" time, they're where task-switching actually happens.
6. Mark which flexible tasks are must-do-today versus nice-to-do-today, and place the nice-to-haves at the end so the day's structure doesn't collapse if something runs long.
7. Keep the total plan visibly under the available hours, not filling every minute - end with intentional slack rather than a packed schedule that breaks on first delay.

## Output format

```
TODAY'S PLAN - [date]

ANCHORS (fixed-time, can't move):
- [time] - [commitment]

BLOCKS:
[time range] - [Task type block name, e.g., "Phone/Admin"]
  - [task]
  - [task]
[transition buffer: X min]
[time range] - [Task type block name]
  - [task]
[transition buffer: X min]
...

LOW-ENERGY WINDOW: [time] - scheduled: [light/routine task]

MUST-DO TODAY: [list]
NICE-TO-DO (slides to tomorrow if day runs long): [list]

SLACK LEFT UNSCHEDULED: [amount of time intentionally left open]
```

## Do
- Build the schedule around fixed anchors first, then fit flexible tasks between them.
- Group same-type tasks into blocks even when it reorders "priority" - switching cost is real and worth optimizing for.
- Leave visible unscheduled slack rather than packing the day edge to edge.

## Don't
- Don't schedule high-focus tasks into a known low-energy window just because they're "important."
- Don't treat transition buffers as optional filler to cut when the schedule looks tight - they're structural, not padding.
- Don't silently drop the "nice-to-do" list - keep it visible so it can roll forward instead of disappearing.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Fixed anchors are placed first and flexible tasks fit around them, not the reverse
- [ ] Transition buffers appear between blocks of different task types
- [ ] The plan leaves visible slack rather than filling 100% of available time
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /executive-function-daily-planner or describe the task from the skill description.

# /meeting-prep-script-for-social-anxiety

Builds concrete prep for a specific upcoming meeting or social interaction - what will likely be asked, what to say, and how to exit gracefully - so uncertainty about "what's going to happen" is replaced with an actual plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A specific meeting, appointment, interview, or social gathering is coming up and the anticipation itself - not knowing what will be asked, said, or expected - is the main source of stress, more than the event itself would be if it were already happening.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What the meeting/interaction actually is, who's involved, and the stated purpose
- What specifically feels uncertain or scary about it (being put on the spot, small talk, an unclear agenda, a specific question they expect)
- Whether they want a full script, or just a short list of anchor points to keep nearby
- Whether there's a way to leave early or step out if needed, if that matters to them

## Safety

This is general practical preparation support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice. If anticipatory anxiety about this kind of situation is a frequent, significant source of distress, that's worth mentioning to a therapist or counselor separately - this skill only prepares for the one specific event at hand.

## Workflow

1. Pin down the actual known agenda or purpose of the meeting first, separating what's confirmed from what's being assumed or feared, since a lot of anticipatory anxiety comes from filling unknowns with worst-case guesses.
2. List the two or three most likely questions or topics based on the stated purpose and who's involved, and draft a short, ready answer for each - enough to not be caught blank, not a full script to recite word for word.
3. Prepare one or two neutral opening lines for starting the interaction (a greeting, a small-talk opener) so the first thirty seconds - often the hardest part - is already decided.
4. If small talk or unstructured time is expected, prepare two or three go-to topics or questions to redirect to, so silence or improvisation isn't required.
5. Identify one thing the person can do if they get stuck or overwhelmed mid-conversation (a pause phrase like "let me think about that for a second," a bathroom break, a note to follow up in writing after) and write it as an exact line.
6. If leaving early or stepping out is a real option and matters to them, write one exact exit line they can use without over-explaining.
7. Keep the whole prep short enough to glance at right before the meeting - a wall of text defeats the purpose.

## Output format

```
PREP FOR: [meeting/interaction] with [who] - [date/time]
Known agenda: [confirmed purpose]
What's actually uncertain (vs. assumed worst case): [...]

LIKELY QUESTIONS/TOPICS + READY ANSWERS
- Q: [likely question] → A: "[short ready answer]"
- Q: [likely question] → A: "[short ready answer]"

OPENING LINE:
"[exact words to start]"

IF SMALL TALK HAPPENS - GO-TO TOPICS
- [topic/question to bring up]

IF YOU GET STUCK MID-CONVERSATION
"[exact pause/buy-time line]"

EXIT LINE (if you need to leave or end it)
"[exact words]"
```

## Do
- Separate confirmed facts about the meeting from anxious guesses about what might happen.
- Keep prepared answers short and flexible, not a memorized script to recite verbatim.
- Include a real pause/buy-time line - having permission to pause is often what reduces the fear of being caught off guard.

## Don't
- Don't catastrophize or list every possible worst-case question - stick to the two or three genuinely most likely ones.
- Don't write answers so rigid that a slightly different phrasing of the question would throw the person off.
- Don't skip the exit line if the person indicated it matters to them - having an out reduces anxiety even if it's never used.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Confirmed facts and anxious assumptions about the meeting are clearly separated
- [ ] At least one concrete pause/buy-time line is included
- [ ] The whole prep is short enough to review in under a minute right before the event
```

# /overwhelm-triage-checklist

Takes an unsorted dump of everything currently weighing on someone and sorts it fast into "needs attention in the next hour," "needs attention today," and "can genuinely wait" - with permission to set the rest down for now.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

This is for the moment things have piled up to the point of shutdown - multiple tasks, unread messages, and worries all competing for attention at once - and the person needs a fast sort to find the one or two things that actually need doing right now, not a full planning session.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- A quick, unfiltered dump of everything on their mind right now - encourage listing it exactly as it comes, no need to organize first
- Any genuinely fixed deadline in the next 24 hours, if known
- How much time/capacity they actually have available right now (5 minutes vs. the rest of the day)

## Safety

This is general organizational support for in-the-moment overwhelm, not therapy or medical/psychological advice. If what's being described sounds like a mental health crisis rather than task overwhelm, gently suggest reaching out to a person or professional they trust, and do not attempt to manage that as a task-sorting problem.

## Workflow

1. Take the raw dump exactly as given - do not ask the person to reorganize or clarify it first, since that itself is a barrier in an overwhelmed state.
2. Sort each item into exactly one of three bins: Next Hour (true time-sensitivity or actively causing distress by being unresolved), Today (needs to happen but has no urgent clock), or Can Wait (no real consequence to delaying it further, even if it feels urgent).
3. Default ambiguous items to "Today" or "Can Wait" rather than "Next Hour" - the triage's job is to shrink the urgent pile, not inflate it.
4. For the Next Hour bin, pick exactly one item to act on first, chosen by smallest-effort-to-resolve rather than largest-emotional-weight, since a fast small win reduces overwhelm faster than tackling the heaviest item first.
5. For everything sorted into Today or Can Wait, state explicitly that it has been captured and is not being lost - the point of the list is to make it safe to stop thinking about those items right now.
6. If a worry (not a task) appears in the dump - something with no concrete action attached - sort it into its own "Not a task right now" bin rather than forcing it into the action bins, and note it's fine to just name it without resolving it.
7. Close with a single plain instruction: what to do right this minute, and permission to stop there.

## Output format

```
TRIAGE - [time]

NEXT HOUR (true urgency or actively distressing)
- [item]
→ START HERE: [the one smallest-effort item to act on first]

TODAY (needs doing, no immediate clock)
- [item]

CAN WAIT (captured, not lost - safe to set down for now)
- [item]

NOT A TASK RIGHT NOW (worries without a concrete action)
- [item]

DO RIGHT NOW: [one plain instruction]
Then you can stop. Everything else is written down and not going anywhere.
```

## Do
- Sort fast and default to the calmer bin when an item is ambiguous.
- Pick the "start here" item by ease, not by emotional weight or perceived importance.
- Explicitly separate worries with no concrete action from actual tasks - naming a worry without a fix attached is valid and doesn't need to be solved right now.

## Don't
- Don't ask the person to pre-organize their dump before triaging it - take it as given.
- Don't over-populate the "Next Hour" bin - if everything is urgent, the triage has failed at its one job.
- Don't attempt to resolve or give advice on items in the "Not a task right now" bin - just acknowledge and set them aside.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] The Next Hour bin is small - genuinely urgent items only
- [ ] Exactly one "start here" item is named, chosen for ease not emotional weight
- [ ] Worries without a concrete task are separated out, not forced into an action bin
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /overwhelm-triage-checklist or describe the task from the skill description.

  Rewrites a confusing, jargon-heavy, or vague piece of text (email, notice, instructions) into direct plain language stating exactly what it means and what action, if any, is required. # /plain-language-reframe

Takes a piece of confusing text and rewrites it as a short, literal statement of what it says, what it means, and what (if anything) the reader needs to do - removing tone-guessing and hidden implications.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone has received a piece of text - a work email, a landlord notice, a school letter, a vague text message - and the wording, tone, or indirect phrasing is making it hard to tell what's actually being said or whether a response is expected.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The exact text to reframe (paste it in full - don't paraphrase before sending)
- Who sent it and in what context, if known (helps distinguish a real deadline from a routine FYI)
- Whether the person specifically wants to know "is this asking me to do something" versus a full breakdown of every line

## Safety

This is general organizational/communication support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice, and not legal advice if the document has legal weight (in that case, plain-language reframing is a starting point only - a professional should confirm anything with real consequences).

## Workflow

1. Read the text once and identify its single core purpose in one plain sentence - what is this actually about, stripped of tone and pleasantries.
2. List anything implied but not stated outright - polite indirectness, passive-aggressive phrasing, or corporate/institutional softening - and state plainly what it likely means, marking clearly when it's an inference rather than a direct statement.
3. Extract any explicit or implied deadline, and state it as a plain date/timeframe, calling out if none was actually given despite the text feeling urgent.
4. State clearly whether a response or action is required at all - many messages that feel urgent are FYI-only, and saying so explicitly is often the most useful part of the reframe.
5. If action is required, state exactly what it is in one line, separate from the explanation of what the message meant.
6. Keep the original text's factual content intact - this is a translation of tone and structure, not a summary that drops details that might matter.

## Output format

```
WHAT THIS IS ABOUT (one sentence):
[plain statement]

WHAT IT'S REALLY SAYING (line by line, only where indirect):
- "[quoted or paraphrased original phrase]" → means: [plain meaning] [inferred/stated]

DEADLINE: [date/timeframe, or "none stated"]

DOES THIS NEED A RESPONSE: [Yes / No / Unclear]

IF YES - WHAT TO DO:
[one concrete action]
```

## Do
- Mark clearly which parts are direct restatement versus your inference about implied meaning.
- Say plainly when nothing is actually required, if that's the honest read - reducing false urgency is often the whole point.
- Keep the original wording quoted alongside the plain-language version so the person can check it against their own read.

## Don't
- Don't add reassurance or emotional commentary about how the person should feel about the message.
- Don't guess at a sender's intent as fact - flag it as an inference.
- Don't drop factual details (numbers, names, dates) in the interest of simplifying - simplify structure and tone, not content.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Inferred meaning is clearly distinguished from directly stated meaning
- [ ] "Does this need a response" is answered explicitly, not left implicit
- [ ] No factual detail from the original text was dropped
```

# /routine-change-transition-plan

Maps exactly what will change about a person's routine during an upcoming transition, and builds a concrete lead-up plan so the change is anticipated in detail rather than arriving as one large unknown shift.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A known routine-disrupting change is coming - a move, a new job or school, a schedule shift, holiday travel, a household member leaving or arriving - and the uncertainty of "everything is about to be different" is itself the hard part, more than any single element of the change.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What the change is and roughly when it starts
- What parts of the current routine matter most to the person (specific times, specific objects, specific rituals) so the plan can protect or intentionally replace them rather than letting them silently disappear
- What's already known/confirmed about the new routine versus what's still unknown
- Whether there's any flexibility in how the transition happens (gradual vs. sudden, some control over timing) or whether it's fixed

## Safety

This is general organizational/practical support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice. It helps plan around a known life change; it doesn't address the emotional processing of the change itself, which may be worth discussing with a trusted person or professional separately.

## Workflow

1. List the current routine elements that are actually at stake - specific times, objects, people, sensory anchors (a particular mug, a walk at a particular time, a weekly call) - since vague dread often turns out to be about several very specific losses, not the change in the abstract.
2. For each element, state plainly whether it continues unchanged, changes in a known way, or is genuinely unknown right now - don't guess at unknowns as if they were settled.
3. For anything genuinely unknown, identify one concrete way to find out in advance (a question to ask, a place to look, a person to contact) rather than leaving it as an open worry.
4. For anything that will definitely change, propose one small way to carry a piece of the old routine into the new context if the person wants continuity (e.g., keeping the same morning drink, the same phone background, a call with the same person at a new time) - mark this as optional, not a fix for the whole change.
5. Build a short lead-up timeline with a few concrete preparation actions between now and the change date, spaced out rather than crammed into the last day.
6. If any flexibility exists in how the transition happens, name the options plainly so the person can choose rather than defaulting to whatever happens automatically.
7. Add a plan for the first day/week of the new routine specifically, since the first exposure to a changed routine is usually the highest-friction point.

## Output format

```
TRANSITION: [what's changing] - starts [date]

WHAT'S ACTUALLY CHANGING
| Routine element | Status | Detail |
|-----------------|--------|--------|
| [element] | Unchanged / Changing (known) / Unknown | [detail or "find out by: ..."] |

CONTINUITY OPTIONS (optional, only if wanted)
- [small carried-over element]

LEAD-UP TIMELINE
- [date/milestone]: [prep action]
- [date/milestone]: [prep action]

FLEXIBILITY AVAILABLE (if any)
- [choice point and options]

FIRST DAY/WEEK PLAN
- [specific plan for the highest-friction early exposure]
```

## Do
- Get specific about which exact routine elements are at stake rather than treating "the change" as one undifferentiated thing.
- Clearly separate known changes from genuine unknowns, and give a concrete way to resolve each unknown.
- Give extra attention to the first day/week of the new routine - that's usually where the most friction actually lands.

## Don't
- Don't guess at unknown details and present them as settled - mark them as unknown and give a path to find out.
- Don't insist on continuity elements if the person didn't ask for them - offer, don't prescribe.
- Don't cram all preparation into the day before the change - space it out across the lead-up window.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every routine element is marked unchanged/known-change/unknown, not left vague
- [ ] Each genuine unknown has a concrete way to resolve it before the change happens
- [ ] The first day/week of the new routine has its own specific plan
```

# /self-advocacy-message-draft-for-workplace-accommodation

Drafts a professional, direct message requesting a specific workplace accommodation - stating the functional need and the concrete adjustment requested, at whatever level of personal disclosure the person is comfortable with.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone needs to ask a manager, HR, or a school/program administrator for a specific accommodation (schedule flexibility, written instructions instead of verbal, a quieter workspace, noise-cancelling headphones allowed, extended deadlines) and wants help finding the right words - professional, clear, and not over-explaining.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific accommodation being requested, in concrete terms
- Who the message is going to (direct manager, HR, a formal accommodation process) since that changes tone and whether a formal process/paperwork is likely involved
- How much personal/medical detail the person wants to disclose - default to the minimum necessary unless they say they want to share more
- Whether this is a first request or a follow-up to something already discussed verbally

## Safety

This is general communication/drafting support, not legal advice. Formal workplace accommodation processes (e.g., disability accommodation law in the person's jurisdiction) have specific legal protections and procedures - if the message is part of a formal legal accommodation request, or if it gets a negative response, suggest checking the relevant employment/disability rights process or an advocate/professional in that area rather than relying on this draft alone.

## Workflow

1. State the accommodation being requested in one concrete, specific sentence first - what exactly is being asked for, not a general description of a struggle.
2. Frame the need in functional terms (what helps the person do their job well) rather than requiring a full personal/medical narrative - default to minimal disclosure unless the person explicitly wants to include more.
3. If the person wants to name a condition or diagnosis, include it exactly as they phrase it - never suggest a label or diagnosis they haven't already named themselves.
4. State the request as a clear, reasonable ask, not an apology or a justification-heavy plea - confidence in tone matters as much as content.
5. If this is a formal accommodation process (HR, disability services), note plainly that formal documentation or a doctor's note may be requested as part of that process, without assuming it will be.
6. Offer to discuss further or provide more information if needed, so the message opens a conversation rather than reading as a one-shot ultimatum.
7. Keep the message short enough to read in under thirty seconds - a long message dilutes the actual ask.

## Output format

```
Subject: Request for [accommodation] - [your name]

Hi [recipient name],

I'm writing to request [specific accommodation], which would help me [functional benefit - do X more effectively/reliably].

[Optional, only if person wants to disclose]: This is related to [condition/reason, in their own words].

[If applicable] I understand there may be a formal process or documentation involved - happy to provide whatever's needed.

I'm glad to discuss this further if useful. Thank you for considering it.

[Name]
```

## Do
- Lead with the concrete request, not a lengthy explanation of the struggle behind it.
- Use only the level of personal/medical disclosure the person explicitly chooses - default to less, not more.
- Keep the tone matter-of-fact and confident, treating the request as reasonable rather than as a favor being begged for.

## Don't
- Don't suggest a diagnosis or label the person hasn't already used themselves.
- Don't over-justify or apologize repeatedly within the message - one clear ask is stronger than a defensive one.
- Don't promise a specific legal outcome or guarantee the accommodation will be granted - this is a request, not a determination.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] The specific accommodation requested appears in the first sentence
- [ ] Disclosure level matches exactly what the person said they're comfortable with, no more
- [ ] No guarantee of outcome or unrequested diagnosis/label appears anywhere
```

# /sensory-friendly-event-brief

Builds a short, concrete brief on the likely sensory conditions of a specific event or venue - sound, light, crowd density, exits - plus a practical prep list, so the person can plan around it instead of being surprised by it.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone is deciding whether to attend, or how to prepare for, a specific event or venue (concert, wedding, conference, family gathering, medical waiting room) and wants to know what the actual sensory conditions are likely to be so they can plan accommodations rather than discover problems on arrival.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- What the event/venue is, and any details already known (venue name, event type, expected size, indoor/outdoor)
- What specifically tends to be hardest for this person (loud sudden noise, bright/flickering light, crowding, unpredictable schedule, unfamiliar food/smell) - ask if not stated, since the brief should target their actual triggers, not a generic list
- Whether they're deciding whether to go at all, or already committed and preparing

## Safety

This is general organizational/practical support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice, and not a substitute for direct communication with the venue/event host about specific accommodation needs.

## Workflow

1. State plainly what's actually known about the venue/event versus what's a reasonable general assumption (e.g., "most weddings have a loud reception with amplified music during dancing" is a general pattern, not a confirmed fact about this specific one).
2. Build the brief around the person's stated specific triggers first - don't produce a generic sensory checklist if they told you noise is the only real issue.
3. Note predictable high-intensity moments (e.g., toasts with mic feedback, a countdown/fireworks moment, a crowded entrance rush) so they're expected rather than sudden.
4. Identify realistic mitigation options: quiet/low-stimulation spaces likely to exist (a lobby, an outdoor area, a car), timing strategies (arriving early before crowds build, leaving before the loudest segment), and physical aids (earplugs, sunglasses, a fidget item, noise-cancelling headphones).
5. If they're deciding whether to go at all, lay out the tradeoff plainly - what they'd get from attending versus the realistic sensory cost - without pushing them toward either choice.
6. If a direct question to the host/venue would resolve real uncertainty (e.g., "is there a quiet room," "what time does the loud part start"), suggest the exact question to ask, phrased plainly and without over-explaining the reason.
7. Keep the whole brief concrete and skimmable - this is a reference to check before and during the event, not an essay to read once.

## Output format

```
SENSORY BRIEF - [Event/venue name]
Known vs. assumed: [what's confirmed] / [what's a general pattern, not confirmed]

LIKELY HIGH-INTENSITY MOMENTS
- [moment] - [why it's likely intense] - [roughly when]

YOUR SPECIFIC TRIGGERS ADDRESSED
- [trigger]: [what to expect] → [mitigation option]

QUIET/LOW-STIMULATION OPTIONS LIKELY AVAILABLE
- [space or strategy]

WHAT TO BRING
- [item]

QUESTION TO ASK THE HOST/VENUE (if useful)
"[exact question to ask]"

IF DECIDING WHETHER TO GO
[Plain tradeoff - no pressure either direction]
```

## Do
- Distinguish clearly between confirmed facts about this specific venue and general assumptions about "events like this."
- Target the brief at the person's actual stated triggers rather than a generic sensory checklist.
- Offer a specific, low-awkwardness question they could ask the host/venue directly if it would resolve real uncertainty.

## Don't
- Don't state assumptions about the venue as if they were confirmed facts.
- Don't push the person toward attending or not attending - lay out the tradeoff and let them decide.
- Don't pad the brief with generic sensory-accommodation advice unrelated to their stated triggers.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Confirmed facts and general assumptions are clearly labeled as different
- [ ] The brief addresses the person's specific stated triggers, not a generic list
- [ ] No pressure toward attending or skipping is present if they're deciding
```

- Don't state assumptions about the venue as if they were confirmed facts.
- Don't push the person toward attending or not attending - lay out the tradeoff and let them decide.
- Don't pad the brief with generic sensory-accommodation advice unrelated to their stated triggers.

# /task-breakdown-assistant

Takes one task that feels too big or too vague to start and turns it into a short sequence of concrete, physically-doable steps, each small enough to begin without more planning.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Someone has a task named in their head ("clean the apartment," "deal with the mail pile," "start the work report") but the size or vagueness of it is what's blocking them - not lack of desire to do it. This is for the moment of "I know I need to do this and I still can't start."

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The task, in whatever words the person used - don't ask them to reframe it first
- Roughly how much time is available right now (10 minutes vs. an open afternoon changes the shape of the plan)
- Any part of the task already done or any constraint that rules out an approach (e.g., "can't do anything loud, roommate is sleeping")

## Safety

This is general organizational support, not therapy or medical/psychological advice. It's a practical planning tool, not a diagnosis, treatment, or clinical recommendation.

## Workflow

1. Restate the task in one plain sentence to confirm the actual target, without adding scope the person didn't mention.
2. Identify the true first physical action - not "plan the cleaning," but "pick up the three cups on the desk and carry them to the sink." The first step must require zero decision-making to begin.
3. Break the rest of the task into steps small enough that each one is a single visible action with an obvious end point (a room, a surface, a pile, a screen) - not abstract phases.
4. Order steps by what unblocks the next one, not by "logical" category - e.g., clearing floor space before vacuuming, not alphabetizing by room.
5. Attach a rough time estimate to each step, and mark which steps are safe to stop after if energy runs out, versus which one should be finished once started (rare - most steps should be interruptible).
6. If a time constraint was given, mark the steps that fit in that window and note what's genuinely deferred versus dropped.
7. End with a one-line "you can stop after any step and this is still progress" reminder - no guilt framing for partial completion.

## Output format

```
TASK: [restated task]
Time available: [given, or "not specified - plan assumes flexible time"]

FIRST STEP (start here, no decisions needed):
- [concrete action] (~[time])

THEN:
1. [concrete action] (~[time]) [stop-safe / finish-once-started]
2. [concrete action] (~[time]) [stop-safe / finish-once-started]
3. ...

IF TIME RUNS OUT: stopping after step [N] still counts as real progress.
DEFERRED (not part of this session): [anything explicitly out of scope for now]
```

## Do
- Make the very first step small enough to do in under two minutes with zero planning.
- Use concrete nouns (cups, floor, inbox, first paragraph) instead of category words (tidy, organize, handle).
- Mark most steps as safe to stop after - momentum matters more than finishing everything in one sitting.

## Don't
- Don't add motivational language, guilt, or urgency framing - describe steps neutrally.
- Don't expand the task's scope beyond what was named (if they said "clean the desk," don't silently include the whole room).
- Don't turn this into a rigid schedule with fixed clock times unless asked - relative order and rough duration are enough.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] The first step requires no further decision to begin
- [ ] Every step names a concrete, visible action, not an abstract category
- [ ] No guilt, urgency, or motivational filler language appears anywhere
```
